item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite time machine twists in all of fiction, perfectly executed in the SCPverse. Love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The old SCP-100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the original 2008 SCP-100 was the PortaPotty of doom

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently Universe is considered flat

Yes but also no.

The universe is flat, but not by the conventional definition of flat. The universe is not flat in that it is 2 dimensional (like a plane). It is flat in the sense that no two parallel lines drawn on it in any direction will ever intersect. This doesn't mean it's flat like a piece of paper. It means it never wraps back up on itself. It's a symptom of the universe being apparently infinite (thanks to expansion).

The key words above being "apparently". The observable universe as we currently know it has an edge, a limit to what we can see. That edge is expanding rapidly, but it is an edge nonetheless. That allows the observable universe to have a practical center, the point from which all light, energy, and matter began and expanded outwards from.

EDIT: It is also worth mentioning that we have not observed evidence that the universe has any topography, but that doesn't mean that it doesn't have any at all. There's a decent chance that the universe could be a torus, or Mobius, or Klein, or any of the other 15 topographies, yet it's just on a scale too large for us to observe at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Erm, no. Currently Universe is considered flat and since donut is a more complex topological structure, infinite plane was chosen as shape. For an infinite plane, concept of center is meaningless. Also, speaking about space outside of the Universe is weird when the Universe, by definition, includes all of the space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did, and rather enjoyed it too, ya nerd :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hold up a second….
Someone actually read one of my nerd rants?
I was not prepared for this shocking twist!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When things go back in time thanks to this machine, I consider them to have lost all relative motion with regards to the universe, given that they disappear and then reappear completely unmoved. The relative center of the universe pointed out by Wishun in their comment is the (0, 0) of this anomaly's grid, and coupling that with the Earth's movement itself would mean that the dice popped back into existence at the fixed point from which it disappeared with no prior velocity at all, while the Earth and the greater universe are still moving at rapid speeds. The inertia of the now decelerated object would mean that Earth slammed into the object, not the other way around.

I simply described the series of events as they would have appeared to the Foundation from their perspective, not how they were actually mechanically taking place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me a while to get this one but it's been growing on me overnight. It's another of those articles that shines later on once the beans have been spilled. I like this.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why Test 3272-3 would register a "high-velocity impact" though. Even considering the centripetal acceleration of the Earth spinning on its axis, and spinning around the Sun, a picosecond would not be nearly enough to change the relative momentum at all. The displacement would be about 30 nanometers, the change in relative velocity would be… way, way smaller.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heheheh. Clever. +1

item.title: The Epitome of a Shitty Superpower
item.content:encoded:
Pull a legendary move by calculating the exact time in the past or future where the earth will be in the exact same position, with the exact same momentum, and bam, you've got yourself a box that can take you to the extremely distant future or past for absolutely no reason.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank God, an SCP that's short, sweet, and leaves the reader with unsettling questions.

My only, tiny nit: The use of the phrase "reenter" in the first description belies the assumption that these corpses are returning to Earth, or originated from it. I don't think a good Foundation researcher would make that assumption lightly - I'd prefer the word "enter".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess so, yeah. But the bodies did come from earth originally so it's right in retrospect. Honestly it was more of an artistic decision based on the way it sounded and flowed as opposed to accuracy. I guess it's just a balance you've gotta walk when making SCPs, ya know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it! The only complaint I have about this SCP is that in the old SCP file, the bodies were described to reenter earths atmosphere. Since it was believed they originated from space, shouldn't it be enter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a slight suspicion what this could be in the outdated version. The first lines of the description cemented my guess. Not that it's a bad thing, I enjoyed the article and the length and experiments were appropriate to the idea, and the hints are obviously there to allow the reader to connect the dots before the article ends.

+1

item.title: +100 in 6 days!
item.content:encoded:
It's raining men! Hallelujah!
It's raining men! Every specimen!

TALL, BLONDE, DARK, AND LEAN

ROUGH AND TOUGH AND STRONG AND MEAN!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Every space has a center.

What the linked article forgets is that "center" is not a matter of common sense.
It's a very precisely defined technical term.

I'll spare you the extremely nerdy details, but the important part is that the center of a set of points is not necessarily part of that set.

In the inflating balloon analogy, the two dimensional surface of the balloon is equivalent to the expanding three dimensions of space in the universe. The center of the balloon is not on the elastic surface. It's in a different direction entirely, inside where the air is contained, and inaccessible from the surface.

Similarly, the center of the universe is not necessarily a place in space we could fly a rocket to. Current observations as laid out in that article strongly suggest it would be a point laying on a theoretical fourth directional access. That direction would be at 90 degrees from the three we know of, and may or may not be "real" in the practical sense of the word. But the center of the universe "exists" at least as much as the black holes we can reason are out there but can't actually see.

This has been your math nerd moment nobody asked for. We now return you to your regularly scheduled wiki reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no such center of the universe. The Big Bang originated everywhere. Earth is equally the center of the universe as much as anywhere else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Relative to the center of the universe, as in, the point from which the big bang originated. Everything is expanding from there. That's the logical (0, 0) to me anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The real question here is, relative to what? If it's not relative to the porta-potty, what is it relative to? This always bothers me with this type of thing. It feels like the piece is mocking humans for being Earthcentric, but there's no logic to being otherwise centered, and the object clearly has some center. In fact, by the distances given, the center must be part of the known universe.

Anyway, good article, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't there an existing SCP that has an almost identical concept to this? (some Prometheus Labs teleporting device that doesn't work properly) Was that SCP-100? I'm sure it was more recently on the wiki than 2012 when the current SCP-100 was posted, though.

I'm strongly inclined to downvote this because like I said I'm 99% sure this already exists on the wiki. Not being able to find the original article made me leave it as a novote.

item.title: Bravo
item.content:encoded:
Really love this Skip.
Format and delivery elevate the peice greatly. Well thought out.
We are all slaves to relativity.
Just hope I can produce something even half as good.
GG

item.title: Re: I don't think I get it.
item.content:encoded: scp articles like this one make me feel so stupid my brain just doesn't get it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very tasty piece of time-travel shenanigan. I couldn't stop myself from grinning when I saw the start of the second description — the whole thing's very nicely executed, with not too much fluff at the end. I'm not sure the tests are necessary, but I can also see why you'd want to keep them, and they don't harm my enjoyment enough to stop me upvoting either way. Very well done :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: isn't science terrifying

item.title: Re: I don't think I get it.
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for explaining that to me, I'm not an expert on how time in space works, lol.

I now see why people like this one so much, and rightfully so! +1

item.title: Re: I don't think I get it.
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

The Earth is moving around the sun, and the sun is moving around the center of the Galaxy, et cetera, et cetera. As such, this is a time machine, but not a spacetime machine. Since the Earth has moved since or previous to you moving in time, you get dumped off into empty space, and, in the case of people going to the past, you end up floating in the current trajectory of Earth, so you fall down like a shooting star.

item.title: I don't think I get it.
item.content:encoded: I'm assuming what this machine does, is that it sends things into space so that they gain five seconds of time, but are close enough to immediately re-enter Earth again? I don't know, the ending just seemed a bit cryptic to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 10/10 TURDIS is a good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine immediate deceleration from 70+ km/s to zero and then to full speed. That's a lot of power.

E: typo is fixed, many thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2 things:
'Closed timeline curve' should be 'Closed timeliKe curve'.

And

It says it's thought to be incapable of altering the past or future of baseline reality but that's obviously not the case if it's teleporting bodies into space years ago and dice into walls a picosecond from now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[feceslike]

Lmao dude you know you're allowed to swear on this site, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s like a really [feceslike] doctor who.

Was that a pun?

item.title: Re: +50 in 21 hours
item.content:encoded: Don't catch cold!

item.title: +50 in 21 hours
item.content:encoded:
I'M GONNA GO OUT, GONNA LET MYSELF GET

ABSOLUTELY SOAKING WEEEEET!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the old SCP-100 and I like the article itself it's clever but unintentionally conjuring memories of the wiki as it was when I first joined kind of make me love this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except for the time it did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And here I was thinking, at first, that the porto-potty served as a portal to space for a murderer to dispose bodies with. The actual conclusion is a lot better though, and concurring with everyone else on this thread, big +1.

item.title: Re: Big +1
item.content:encoded: I like your rewording better, and have implemented it :)

item.title: Big +1
item.content:encoded:
+ Possible Spoilers
- HIDE

I have loved this concept ever since I saw the original draft, and its execution is basically perfect. It gives away just enough information for a reader to work out what exactly is going on without spelling it out for them. That being said, I have one single phrase that I'm not the biggest fan of:

telescopes with a resolution of 0.02 arc-seconds or less.

Something about this line just rubs me the wrong way. I can't quite put my finger on what it is, it's almost definitely not even grammatically incorrect. I just much prefer phrases like 'telescopes with a resolution of 0.02 or fewer arcseconds1'

Footnotes
1. Also, how is arcsecond meant to be spelled? Wikipedia and several other sources say arcsecond and arc second are both acceptable, but spellcheck insists that it's hyphenated. Weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great work. The changes you've made to the last two logs really solidify the pay-off. The inclusion of momentum, impact, and a tie-in event to the first part of the article seals it all in. It's clear, but not obvious what's going on. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate this is what should always happen but it never does

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not gonna get another S4 page ever, am I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like a really shitty doctor who.

I'm not going to spoil this in the discussion because from what I remember like half the people you surveyed with this didn't get the twist/joke, but it still makes me smile. I wonder if they'll ever find Monette, or if he's lying in a field somewhere? Would you even be able to tell after all that?

item.title: Alternate Title: 70km/s
item.content:encoded:
Humidity is rising Mm rising barometer's getting low How low, girl?
According to all sources What sources now?
The street's the place to go You better hurry up
'Cause tonight for the first time
Just about half-past ten
For the first time in history
It's gonna start raining men

~ The Weather Girls

This was a total fucking blast to write, it practically flew off the keyboard.

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

The porta potty is a time machine, but not a spacetime machine ;D

Special thanks to my critters:
MaliceAforethought, Crashington, gee0765, T Rutherford, Veiedhimaedhr, Ihp, Jekeled, Pr10r, Swaghetti, rattles, and Roget.

+ Image Attribution
- Image Attribution
